psychology.title: Neurological Escapism
psychology.subtitle: The Cognitive Flight Mechanism
psychology.content: Escapism in psychology is defined as a 'dissociative state' used to mitigate chronic stress. While 'restorative escapism' (like reading) builds resilience, 'avoidant escapism'—like Charley’s obsession—leads to Maladaptive Daydreaming. This is a neurological attempt to bypass the Prefrontal Cortex's stress signals by flooding the brain with dopamine-rich nostalgia.
points: Avoidance Coping: Denying reality to reduce immediate anxiety.
The Nostalgia Filter: The brain's tendency to prune negative memories from the past.
Cognitive Dissonance: The tension Charley feels between his modern life and his 'truth'.

comparison.title: Temporal Stress Analysis
comparison.subtitle: 1894 Industrialism vs. 2026 Digitalism
modern_stress: Algorithmic Anxiety: The pressure of constant social comparison.
Digital Burnout: The blurring of work-life boundaries due to 24/7 connectivity.
Eco-Anxiety: Modern global stressors that did not exist in the 19th century.
vintage_stress: Neurasthenia: A 19th-century term for 'nerve exhaustion' from the new pace of steam power.
Post-War Trauma: Lingering societal scars from the Civil War era.
Industrial Rigidity: Extreme physical labor and total lack of workplace safety.

history.title: Grand Central: The Labyrinth
history.subtitle: Architectural Liminality
history.content: Grand Central Terminal is a 'liminal space'—a place of transition where time feels suspended. Built on a celestial map (the zodiac ceiling), it represents the human desire to connect the mundane (trains) with the eternal (stars). In Finney's narrative, the station is a biological entity, a 'tree' growing roots into the past to save those drowning in the present.

verdict.title: Solution or Fatal Illusion?
verdict.subtitle: The Final Verdict
verdict.content: The Third Level is a fatal siren song. To accept the 1890s as a 'solution' is to ignore the progress of medicine and human rights. Charley does not find a solution; he finds a hiding spot. True psychological health requires 'Active Adaptation'—changing the present—rather than 'Passive Regression' into a fictionalized past.
